text.title: EDA Core Patterns
text.summary: Pub/Sub, Event Sourcing, CQRS, and Saga patterns — rules, use cases, and trade-offs.

section.title: Pub/Sub

rule.statement: Publishers emit events to topics without knowledge of subscribers. Subscribers register independently and process at their own pace. The broker decouples the two.
rule.rationale: Producer-consumer awareness creates coupling. When the producer must know about each consumer, adding a new consumer requires a producer change — defeating the extensibility benefit of EDA.

section.p: Use cases: notifications, fan-out processing (one order-placed event → email, inventory, analytics, billing), event distribution to multiple bounded contexts.

rule.statement: Events in EDA are integration events (cross-service); events in event sourcing are domain events (within a service). Do not conflate them. A domain event may or may not be published as an integration event.
rule.rationale: Publishing every internal domain event externally creates tight coupling between a service's internal model and its consumers. Select which domain events become integration contracts.

rule.statement: In EDA, read models are built by consuming events from the write side. The projection is eventually consistent — design UI and APIs to handle this (pending state, polling, websocket updates).
rule.rationale: Assuming immediate consistency in an eventually consistent system causes UI bugs where newly submitted data does not appear in the next page load.

rule.statement: Every saga step must have a written compensating action before the saga is deployed. Compensation runs in reverse order when any step fails.
rule.rationale: A deployed saga without compensations is a data corruption waiting to happen. Step 1 side effects (reservation, charge) become permanent when step 2 fails with no rollback path.

rule.statement: Use orchestration-based sagas when more than 3-4 services participate. Choreography with many services creates spaghetti event flows with no central place to observe state.
rule.rationale: Choreography distributes the saga state machine across services; debugging a failed saga requires tracing events across all participants. Orchestration keeps state in one place.
